FULL CONTENT:
The Ultimate Guide to Online Community Management in 2025

Online communities have become a cornerstone of modern marketing and brand-building strategies. They offer businesses a unique way to connect with customers, provide value, and foster loyalty. In this detailed guide, weâll explore the intricacies of online community management, its evolution, and how to master it for success in 2025.

What is Online Community Management?

Online community management involves creating and maintaining vibrant digital spaces where people engage with your brand, share ideas, and form connections with like-minded individuals. Itâs about more than moderating comments or running social media accountsâitâs about nurturing relationships that drive value for both the business and its community members.

A well-managed online community serves as a hub for discussion, collaboration, and innovation. Community managers play a vital role in fostering these interactions by:

Effective community management builds a sense of belonging, turning casual participants into loyal advocates for your brand.

Why Online Community Management Matters

1. Enhances Brand Loyalty

When customers feel valued and connected to a brand, they are more likely to remain loyal. A thriving community fosters emotional connections, creating advocates who promote your brand organically.

2. Drives Customer Engagement

Engaged customers are more likely to interact with your content, participate in discussions, and provide feedback. This leads to stronger customer relationships and more meaningful interactions.

3. Reduces Customer Support Costs

Communities often become self-sustaining, with members helping one another solve problems. This reduces the burden on customer support teams and accelerates issue resolution.

4. Gathers Actionable Insights

Communities are goldmines of information. By monitoring discussions, businesses can gather valuable insights into customer preferences, pain points, and ideas for improvement.

5. Boosts Brand Visibility

Active online communities generate buzz, increasing your brandâs visibility. Members share their positive experiences, attracting new customers and expanding your reach.

The Evolution of Online Communities

From early forums and chat rooms to todayâs sophisticated platforms, online communities have evolved significantly. Initially, these spaces were primarily used for sharing information. Now, they serve as ecosystems for innovation, collaboration, and brand building.

Key Trends Shaping Community Management in 2025

Types of Online Communities

1. Support Communities

These communities focus on helping members solve problems. By enabling peer-to-peer assistance, support communities reduce customer service workloads and improve satisfaction.

2. Product Feedback Communities

These communities encourage members to share ideas and feedback, helping businesses refine their offerings.

3. Advocacy and Acquisition Communities

Advocacy communities leverage loyal customers to promote your brand and attract new members.

4. Content and Contribution Communities

These communities rely on user-generated content to fuel engagement. Members share their work, ideas, or experiences, creating a dynamic and interactive environment.

5. Engagement Communities

Engagement communities focus on building connections among members, often around shared interests or hobbies.

6. Internal Communities

Internal communities facilitate collaboration and knowledge sharing within organizations.

Steps to Build a Thriving Online Community

1. Define Your Objectives

Clearly outline what you aim to achieve with your community. Whether itâs increasing engagement, gathering feedback, or building brand loyalty, your goals will shape your strategy.

2. Identify Your Target Audience

Understand the demographics, preferences, and behaviors of your audience. Use surveys and analytics to tailor your community to their needs.

3. Choose the Right Platform

Select a platform that aligns with your goals and audience preferences. Consider options like Facebook Groups, Discord, or a dedicated forum on your website.

4. Develop a Content Strategy

Content is the backbone of any community. Create a mix of educational, entertaining, and interactive content to keep members engaged.

5. Set Clear Guidelines

Establish rules to maintain a respectful and inclusive environment. Clearly communicate these guidelines to all members.

6. Foster Engagement

Encourage participation through discussions, polls, events, and rewards. Recognize active members to motivate others.

7. Monitor and Moderate

Actively monitor discussions to ensure compliance with guidelines. Use moderation tools to address issues promptly.

8. Measure Success

Track metrics such as engagement rates, member growth, and content performance to evaluate your communityâs impact.

Best Practices for Online Community Management

The Role of Technology in Community Management

Technology is transforming how communities are managed. Here are some essential tools:

1. AI-Powered Moderation Tools

AI tools like Bevyâs moderation feature automatically identify and remove inappropriate content, ensuring a safe environment.

2. Analytics Platforms

Analytics tools track member activity, providing insights into engagement patterns and areas for improvement.

3. Communication Tools

Platforms like Slack and Discord facilitate real-time interactions, making communication seamless and efficient.

Measuring Success in Online Communities

Key Metrics to Track

Using Insights to Improve

Regularly review metrics to identify strengths and areas for improvement. Use this data to refine your strategy and enhance the member experience.

Inspiring Examples of Successful Communities

1. Nike Run Club

Nikeâs app connects runners worldwide, encouraging them to share achievements and stay motivated.

2. HubSpotâs The Spot

This community promotes diversity and inclusion while providing valuable resources for professionals.

3. Salesforceâs Trailblazer Community

Salesforceâs platform empowers users to share insights, learn, and grow together.

Conclusion

Online community management is more than a marketing strategyâitâs a way to build meaningful connections and drive long-term success. By implementing the strategies and best practices outlined in this guide, you can create thriving communities that benefit both your brand and its members.

For more insights and tools, explore Bevyâs suite of community management solutions. Together, letâs create communities that inspire, engage, and grow.

--- SOURCE 2: Strategy + Tips for Community Content Management (2024) - Arena ---
URL: https://arena.im/online-communities/online-community-content-management/

SUMMARY:
Online community content management involves creating, organizing, and maintaining content to foster engagement and growth. Effective content management in online communities keeps the community active and helps build a strong, loyal member base. Creating and managing content in an online community isn’t just about posting updates; it’s about fostering a space where members feel valued and engaged. User-generated content fosters a sense of ownership and belonging among members, making the community more vibrant and engaging. Community managers help keep members informed and engaged by providing valuable and informative content. This type of content aims to engage and inform community members while reinforcing the brand’s message and values. Interactive content makes the community more engaging and provides valuable insights into members’ preferences and behaviors.

FULL CONTENT:
[Skip to main content](#ajax-content-wrap)

Managing content in online communities can feel like juggling multiple balls at once. You need to keep your audience engaged, ensure the content is relevant, and maintain a positive environment. Understanding the basics of online community content management can make this task more manageable.

Online community content management involves creating, organizing, and maintaining content to foster engagement and growth. This process includes moderating user-generated content, curating relevant articles, and organizing everything into clear categories.

Let’s dive into what online community content management entails.

## **What is Online Community Content Management?**

It’s about creating, organizing, and maintaining content within online communities to foster engagement, collaboration, and growth. This involves several key activities:

* **Moderating User-Generated Content:** Ensuring that posts, comments, and discussions adhere to community guidelines and maintain a respectful environment.
* **Creating and Curating Relevant Content:** Sharing articles, videos, and resources that are valuable to the community members. This helps keep the community informed and engaged.
* **Organizing Content into Categories and Topics:** Structuring the content in a way that makes it easy for members to find and interact with. This could involve creating specific forums or threads dedicated to different topics.

Effective content management in online communities keeps the community active and helps build a strong, loyal member base. Check out this [comprehensive guide](https://arena.im/audience-engagement/building-thriving-online-communities/) for more insights on building thriving online communities.

## **How to Create a Content Strategy for Your Online Community**

Before jumping into the specifics, it’s crucial to understand why a solid content strategy matters.

### 1. **Define Your Community’s Purpose and Goals**

Start by identifying the main objectives of your online community. Are you aiming to provide a space for users to share knowledge, offer support, or foster a sense of belonging? Clearly defining your purpose helps guide your content strategy. For example, if your goal is to educate, your content might include tutorials, how-to guides, and expert interviews. If the aim is to build a support network, focus on discussion forums and Q&A sessions.

### 2. **Understand Your Audience**

Research your target audience’s interests, needs, and preferences. Use surveys, polls, and analytics tools to gather data on what your members care about. Look at demographic information, but also dig deeper into their behaviors and motivations. What problems are they trying to solve? What types of content do they engage with most? Understanding these aspects helps tailor your content to meet their expectations and keep them engaged. For more on engagement strategies, explore [customer vs audience engagement](https://arena.im/audience-engagement/customer-engagement-vs-audience-engagement/).

### 3. **Determine Content Themes and Categories**

Identify key topics and themes that align with your community’s purpose. Create a list of broad themes that cover the interests and needs of your audience. For instance, a fitness community might have themes like workout routines, nutrition tips, and mental health. Break these themes down into specific categories to organize your content effectively. This structure makes it easier for members to find relevant information and participate in discussions.

### 4. **Establish a Content Calendar**

Plan and schedule content creation and curation using a content calendar. This tool helps you stay organized and ensures a steady flow of content. Decide on the frequency of your posts—daily, weekly, or monthly—depending on your community’s activity level. Include a mix of content types, such as articles, videos, and interactive posts, to keep things fresh. A content calendar also helps you plan around key dates and events, ensuring timely and relevant content.

### 5. **Set Key Performance Indicators (KPIs)**

Define metrics to measure the success of your content strategy. KPIs provide a way to track progress and make data-driven decisions. Common KPIs for online communities include engagement rates, such as likes, comments, and shares, as well as member growth and retention rates. You might also track the performance of specific content types to see what resonates most with your audience. Regularly review these metrics to adjust your strategy and improve outcomes.

## **5 Tips for Effective Online Community Content Management**

Feeling lost on where to start with content management? Here are some practical tips to guide you.

### **Encourage User-Generated Content**

Create opportunities and incentives for members to contribute content. User-generated content brings authenticity and diverse perspectives to your community. Encourage members to share their experiences, ask questions, and provide feedback. You can do this by setting up discussion threads, hosting Q&A sessions, or creating challenges that prompt members to share their stories. Offering incentives like badges, recognition, or even small rewards can motivate members to participate more actively.

### **Maintain a Consistent Brand Voice**

Ensure all content aligns with your brand’s tone and values. Consistency in your brand voice helps build trust and recognition among community members. Whether it’s a post from a community manager or a reply to a member’s comment, the tone should reflect your brand’s personality. Develop a style guide that outlines your brand’s voice, including preferred language, tone, and messaging guidelines. Share this guide with everyone involved in content creation to maintain uniformity across all interactions.

### **Use a Mix of Content Formats**

Incorporate text, images, videos, and interactive content to keep members engaged. Different formats cater to different preferences and can make your community more dynamic. Text posts are great for detailed information, while images and videos can capture attention quickly and convey messages more effectively. Interactive content like polls, quizzes, and challenges can boost engagement by encouraging members to participate actively. Regularly mix these formats to keep the content fresh and appealing.

### **Regularly Update and Refresh Content**

Keep content fresh and relevant to maintain member interest. Stale content can lead to disengagement, so updating and refreshing your community’s content regularly is important. This could mean posting new articles, updating old posts with current information, or introducing new discussion topics. Pay attention to trending topics and incorporate them into your content strategy. Regular updates show that the community is active and evolving, which can help retain member interest.

### **Monitor and Respond to Feedback**

Actively listen to community feedback and adapt your content strategy accordingly. Feedback from your community members provides valuable insights into what works and what doesn’t. Monitor comments, suggestions, and discussions to understand their needs and preferences. Respond to feedback promptly and make necessary adjustments to your content strategy. This shows members that their opinions matter and helps create a more tailored and engaging community experience.

## **Types of Content in Online Communities**

Creating and managing content in an online community isn’t just about posting updates; it’s about fostering a space where members feel valued and engaged.

### **User-Generated Content**

[User-generated content](https://www.semrush.com/blog/user-generated-content/?kw=&cmp=BR_POR_SRCH_DSA_Blog_EN&label=dsa_pagefeed&Network=g&Device=c&utm_content=683768481249&kwid=dsa-2263819780079&cmpid=18361973175&agpid=156031508066&BU=Core&extid=116380656181&adpos=&gad_source=1&gclid=Cj0KCQjwz7C2BhDkARIsAA_SZKYvUF1UZxYhWPZVUVjGiFzfOt2rZ8dKYZueuWGwDHAB8XVbcE4ZvJMaAiVXEALw_wcB) forms the backbone of most online communities. This includes posts, comments, and discussions initiated by community members. These contributions create a dynamic and interactive environment where members can share their experiences, ask questions, and provide feedback. User-generated content fosters a sense of ownership and belonging among members, making the community more vibrant and engaging. Learn how to [increase engagement with user-generated content](https://arena.im/audience-engagement/how-to-increase-revenue-engagement-with-user-generated-content/) to boost your community’s activity.

### **Curated Content**

Curated content involves selecting and sharing relevant articles, videos, and resources. Community managers play a key role in this process, ensuring that the content aligns with the interests and needs of the community. Community managers help keep members informed and engaged by providing valuable and informative content. Curated content can include industry news, educational materials, and other resources that add value to the community. Discover [proven tactics to grow engagement](https://arena.im/audience-engagement/audience-engagement-definition-growth-tactics/) through effective content curation.

### **Branded Content**

Branded content is created by the organization or brand that runs the community. This type of content aims to engage and inform community members while reinforcing the brand’s message and values. Branded content can take various forms, such as blog posts, videos, infographics, and announcements. It helps establish the brand’s authority and fosters a deeper connection between the brand and its community members.

### **Interactive Content**

Interactive content includes polls, quizzes, and challenges designed to encourage participation and engagement. This type of content effectively keeps members active and involved in the community. Polls can gather opinions on various topics, quizzes can test knowledge or provide entertainment, and challenges can motivate members to achieve specific goals. Interactive content makes the community more engaging and provides valuable insights into members’ preferences and behaviors.

## **Benefits of Effective Community Content Management**

Managing content effectively can significantly impact your community’s success, but why should you care?

### **Increased Engagement**

Effective content management keeps your community members active and invested. When you consistently provide relevant and engaging content, members are more likely to participate in discussions, share their thoughts, and contribute their own content. This creates a vibrant and dynamic community where members feel valued and connected. Regular updates and interactive content, such as polls and quizzes, can further boost engagement by encouraging members to interact with the content and each other. For more strategies, explore the [ultimate guide to audience engagement](https://arena.im/audience-engagement/ultimate-guide-to-audience-engagement/).

### **Improved User Experience**

A well-organized community makes it easy for members to find the content and resources they need. By categorizing content into clear topics and sections, you help members navigate the community effortlessly. This improves their overall experience and encourages them to return. Additionally, having a structured content management system ensures that outdated or irrelevant content is regularly updated or removed, keeping the community fresh and relevant. This streamlined experience enhances member satisfaction and retention.

### **Enhanced Brand Loyalty**

Consistent and thoughtful content management builds trust and strengthens the relationship between your brand and community members. When members see that you are committed to providing valuable and relevant content, they are more likely to develop a positive perception of your brand. This trust fosters loyalty, as members feel that their needs and interests are being addressed. Engaging with members through branded content and responding to their feedback also shows that you value their input, further deepening their connection to your brand. Learn how to [optimize funnel conversions](https://arena.im/audience-engagement/how-to-optimize-funnel-conversions-with-engagement/) with effective engagement strategies.

### **Valuable Insights**

Effective content management provides valuable data and feedback that can inform your content strategies and product development. By analyzing which types of content resonate most with your community, you can tailor your future content to better meet their needs. User-generated content and interactions offer insights into member preferences, pain points, and interests. This information can guide your content creation efforts and help you develop products or services that align with what your community values. Regularly reviewing content performance metrics allows you to make data-driven decisions and continuously optimize your content strategy to keep your community thriving.

## **How does Online Community Content Management Work?**

Feeling overwhelmed by the idea of managing your community’s content? You’re not alone, but breaking it down into manageable steps can make a world of difference.

**Developing a content strategy aligned with community goals**   Start by defining what you want to achieve with your community. Your content strategy should align with these goals, whether it’s increasing member engagement, providing support, or fostering discussions. This involves planning the types of content you’ll create, how often you’ll post, and the platforms you’ll use. Learn how to [grow engagement with a virtual community](https://arena.im/audience-engagement/grow-engagement-with-a-virtual-community/) to enhance your content strategy.

**Establishing content guidelines and moderation policies**: Clear guidelines help maintain a positive and respectful environment. Outline what types of content are acceptable, how members should interact, and the consequences of violating the rules. Moderation policies ensure these guidelines are consistently enforced, using both human moderators and automated tools to review content.

**Regularly creating and curating relevant content**: Consistency is key. Regularly post new content that is relevant and valuable to your community. This could include articles, videos, and updates. Curate content from other sources that align with your community’s interests to keep the content diverse and engaging. For real-time engagement strategies, explore [real-time audience engagement](https://arena.im/audience-engagement/real-time-audience-business/).

**Encouraging user-generated content and engagement**: Foster a sense of ownership by encouraging members to contribute their own content. Create opportunities for them to share their experiences, ask questions, and provide feedback. This not only increases engagement but also builds a stronger community.

**Analyzing content performance and user feedback**: Track how your content is performing using analytics tools. Look at metrics like engagement rates, shares, and comments to understand what resonates with your community. Collect feedback from members to gain insights into their preferences and needs. Learn how to [track audience engagement growth](https://arena.im/audience-engagement/strategies-to-track-your-audience-engagement-course-correct/) and course correct effectively.

**Continuously optimizing content based on insights and community needs**: Use the data and feedback you gather to refine your content strategy. Identify what works and what doesn’t, and make adjustments accordingly. This might involve changing the types of content you post the frequency of updates, or the platforms you use. Regularly updating your strategy ensures your community remains relevant and engaging.

## **What are the Best Practices for Online Community Content Moderation?**

Moderation is often a major pain point for community managers, but following best practices can make a big difference.

### **Establish Clear Community Guidelines and Rules**

Set clear and straightforward guidelines for your community. These rules should outline acceptable behavior, the types of content allowed, and the consequences of violating these standards. Make sure these guidelines are easily accessible to all members. Clear rules help maintain order and set expectations for everyone involved.

### **Train Moderators to Enforce Guidelines Consistently**

Consistency is key in moderation. Train your moderators thoroughly so they understand the guidelines and know how to enforce them fairly. Regular training sessions can help keep everyone on the same page and ensure that moderators handle situations uniformly. This consistency builds trust within the community and helps avoid misunderstandings.

### **Use a Combination of Human Moderation and Automated Tools**

Relying solely on human moderators can be overwhelming, especially in large communities. Combine human moderation with automated tools to streamline the process. Automated tools can quickly flag inappropriate content, while human moderators can review and make final decisions. This approach balances efficiency with the nuanced understanding that only humans can provide.

### **Respond Promptly to Reported Content and Member Concerns**

Timely responses to reported content and member concerns show that you value your community’s well-being. Set up a system to ensure that reports are addressed quickly. Prompt action helps prevent issues from escalating and demonstrates that the community is actively managed. It also reassures members that their concerns are taken seriously.

### **Regularly Review and Update Moderation Policies**

Community needs and online behaviors evolve over time. Regularly review and update your moderation policies to keep them relevant. Gather feedback from moderators and community members to identify areas for improvement. Updating policies ensures they remain effective and aligned with the community’s current needs.

### **Foster a Positive and Inclusive Community Culture**

Encourage a culture of respect and inclusivity within your community. Highlight positive interactions and recognize members who contribute constructively. Promote diversity and ensure that all members feel welcome and valued. A positive culture enhances member experience and reduces the likelihood of conflicts and negative behavior.

## **Take Your Online Community Content Management to the Next Level**

Managing content in online communities can be challenging, but with the right tools, you can streamline the process and enhance engagement. Arena offers a comprehensive suite of AI-driven solutions designed to make content management more effective and efficient. From real-time chat and live blogging to interactive polls and quizzes, our platform provides everything you need to keep your community active and engaged.

Don’t let content management overwhelm you. Arena’s advanced features, such as AI moderation and dynamic content updates, ensure your community stays vibrant and relevant. # 10 proven ways to increase online community engagement

Khoros Staff

You’ve worked hard to [build your online community](https://khoros.com/blog/how-to-build-an-online-community) — but online community engagement is where the real challenge begins. Keeping members active and invested takes more than just a great platform; it requires intention, consistency, and the right strategies.

The benefits of online community engagement go beyond just activity — they include stronger relationships, deeper customer loyalty, and increased revenue. But what happens when your community goes quiet?

In this article, we’ll explore what online community engagement really means, the key benefits of getting it right, and how to increase community engagement with 10 practical, proven strategies you can start using today.

## What is online community engagement?

Online community engagement refers to the level of participation, connection, and interaction taking place within your digital space. It's more than just members showing up — true engagement happens when people contribute, connect with others, and return regularly because they find value in the experience.

Successful online engagement doesn't happen by chance. It requires a thoughtful approach and consistent effort to create an environment where members feel heard and involved.

To increase community engagement, start by looking at how people currently interact within your space. Focus on three key areas:

* **How members engage with your brand** – Are they asking questions, providing feedback, or joining brand-led initiatives?
* **How members engage with each other** – Are there ongoing conversations, support threads, or peer-to-peer exchanges?
* **How members engage with your content** – Are they liking, commenting on, or contributing to posts and online community engagement activities?

Evaluating these areas can reveal engagement gaps and help you shape more effective online community engagement activities that deepen relationships and drive long-term loyalty.

## Benefits of online community engagement

A [branded online community](https://khoros.com/blog/brand-community-examples) with consistent, meaningful interaction can unlock advantages that traditional marketing channels often can’t. Here are some of the key benefits of online community engagement:

* **Stronger relationships**: Online community engagement fosters stronger connections between a brand and its customers, building a sense of community and trust.
* **Customer loyalty:** Actively engaging with customers online enhances [customer loyalty](https://khoros.com/blog/using-customer-communities-boost-loyalty-retention) to your brand by creating a more personalized and interactive experience, and loyal customers mean repeat business and increased revenue.
* **Informed decision-making:** With online communities, brands can gather customer insights to make informed decisions based on customer feedback and preferences.
* **Tackle misinformation and debunk myths**: A wonderful feature of online communities is the ability to create and share your own resources, allowing you to address misinformation, correct inaccuracies, and dispel myths effectively.
* **Identify real issues:** An online community can help a brand identify and address genuine concerns, which also allows for timely solutions through monitoring interactions.
* **Generate new leads**: Active participation in online spaces can attract potential customers, expand the brand’s reach and generate new leads.
* **Increase customer retention**: By maintaining a consistent and positive online presence, brands can enhance customer satisfaction, increasing retention rates.

## How to increase community engagement: 10 strategies to implement

Depending on where you’d like to increase engagement in your online community, some tactics may work better than others for specific situations. Here are ten online community engagement strategies to boost member activity:

### 1. Educate your customers on your services or products

It shouldn’t come as much of a surprise that the customer who understands all of the features of your product or service is more likely to use it, recommend it to others, and spend more money on your products in the future. [Forbes](https://www.forbes.com/sites/williamcraig/2015/04/10/dont-market-to-your-customers-educate-them-instead/#592c54fe1cfa) recommends educating your customers instead of marketing to them: “The more informed and empowered customers are, the more satisfied and confident they are with their choices.” Fortunately, online communities provide an excellent space for education about your products or services, including topics related to them.

Sharing resources such as webinars, ebooks, or blog articles can offer customers a way to learn about your product or service benefits them. You can also do this by simply responding to customer questions about how to use your offerings, such as in the example below where a user in the Microsoft Community asks about which features to enable to maximize performance on their device:

Focusing on education rather than marketing builds trust and invites deeper online engagement—keeping members curious, confident, and coming back for more.

### 2. Make members feel special

When members feel recognized, they’re more likely to stick around. Increase community engagement by celebrating wins, spotlighting contributors, and making each member feel seen. [Gamification](https://khoros.com/resources/gamification-brand-engagement) encourages members to share more frequently, increasing overall engagement with your brand. It’s also a critical step in building an online community your customers will love.

Here are a few ways you can make your community members feel special:

* Implement automated gamification tactics for everyday actions, like awarding badges the first time an online community member posts, replies to a post, or gives kudos on another member’s post.
* Create a new member experience to make them feel welcome right away.
* Celebrate your active members and reward them for their contributions. This can be as simple as a personalized message from the community manager or something unique like the Top Helpful Author leaderboard in HP’s online community.

### 3. Leverage user-generated content

User-generated content is one of the most effective online community engagement activities. Customers trust [user-generated content](https://khoros.com/blog/user-generated-content-social-media-strategy) over branded content and are more likely to engage with it. Take your strategy to the next level and boost your online community engagement by creating a vibrant social hub of user-generated content.

Ask your superfans to share video testimonials about how they use your products and how your product makes their lives easier and then share this content within the community. Make sure to also reward your superusers and advocates when they create content such as short-form videos or in-depth how-to articles that provide free support.

### 4. Directly engage disengaged members

Getting inactive users to be involved in the community is a difficult task. But there are tactics that can help these users to re-engage. It’s important to first understand your inactive audience, so you can reach out when the timing is appropriate. Utilizing [branded community software](https://khoros.com/platform/communities) can help you spot disengaged members and get more insight.

Use [community platform tools](https://khoros.com/platform/communities) to identify inactive members, then trigger re-engagement campaigns. Personalized outreach, small calls to action (like polls or “intro yourself” threads), or welcoming them into smaller subgroups can gently draw them back in.

### 5. Gather and act on feedback

[Feedback is crucial](https://khoros.com/blog/feedback-online-community-success) for online community success, and there is no better source of feedback and ideas for improvement than the criticism of your customers. Online communities provide an opportunity to monitor and listen to your customers, helping you identify issues or discover what you’re doing well. Constantly asking for feedback — and acting on it — can turn passive members into active participants.

Do this by continuously soliciting questions and comments and encouraging them to tell you what they dislike and love.

Listening also means knowing when to sit on your hands and wait to respond. When customers have negative comments, it is exponentially more powerful to have their peers to defend your brand rather than your customer service team. If you’ve followed the steps to build stronger relationships through community engagement above, your customers will become your greatest brand advocates.

### 6. Spark conversations with smart questions

As simple as this might sound, focusing on question-based content in your community [unlocks community engagement](https://khoros.com/resources/unlocking-community-engagement). Asking your members what they think instead of always creating content to tell them what you think will prompt great conversations while providing insight for your brand.

Craft discussion prompts that provoke thought and elicit personal experiences, encouraging members to share their perspectives. For instance, in a cooking community, you might ask, “What’s your go-to comfort food, and what memories does it evoke?” This not only sparks conversation but also allows community members to bond over shared culinary experiences.

Also consider incorporating polls or surveys to gather opinions on relevant topics, making members feel their input is valued. And don’t forget to actively participate in discussions sparked by these questions. Respond to any comments with follow-up queries to strengthen individual connections and the community atmosphere.

### 7. Diversify content

Content creation on social media can be limited to one type of content such as only sharing short-form videos. But in an online community, you can mix it up with different forms of content such as:

* Discussion boards
* Photos
* Short-form videos
* Livestreaming
* Polls
* Q&As
* Articles
* All member chats
* Online courses
* Member success stories
* Challenges for members to contribute content

For example, Visa’s Developer Community features Webinars and Blogs in addition to the traditional forum-style content that most people associate with an online community.

This is a great community engagement strategy because not everyone learns in the same way. You can tap into the different interests of your community members by trying out other forms of content. This variety keeps your community fresh, supports different learning styles, and gives members more reasons to stay engaged.

### 8. Create community spaces

Organizing your community into focused spaces gives members a home base for their interests — boosting online engagement and encouraging repeat visits. This segmentation allows members to find and engage with content that aligns with their preferences.

You can implement discussion threads, forums, or channels that focus on niche subjects like interest or even spaces related to specific products and services. Fitbit does a great job of this, with dedicated spaces for each of their products:

Ensure these spaces are moderated to maintain a positive and inclusive environment, encouraging healthy discussions and collaboration among community members.

### 9. Try live streaming

Integrating live streaming into your online community strategy provides real-time interaction — an instant boost in engagement. A great first step to jumping into live video is to identify your top FAQs and answer them in a weekly video series. Live video is a powerful way to humanize your brand and increase real-time community engagement.

Host live Q&A sessions, discussions, or behind-the-scenes glimpses to make members feel more connected. Utilize platforms that support live comments, enabling immediate engagement and feedback. You can also promote live events in advance to build anticipation and attract a larger audience.

### 10. Host events

Organize virtual events your community members can join, such as workshops, webinars, or even scheduled “talk time.” During the events, encourage participation through polls, discussions, or group activities. Hosting regular events not only strengthens community connections but also attracts new members looking for engaging experiences.

Scheduled events help build anticipation and drive recurring traffic to your community. By creating a reliable cadence of events, you give members something to look forward to — turning one-time visitors into regular participants. You can choose a specific discussion topic, invite guest speakers, or prepare a presentation. It’s a fun and easy way to boost engagement.

**Learn more about strengthening relationships with your customers with our** [**Digital Customer Care Playbook,**](https://khoros.com/resources/digital-customer-care-playbook?token=QLq2VJ_WEBBm4MOJOGGI0rgXSLkZ4H0c) **or discover the potential value of your community with the calculator featured below.**

## Fuel customer engagement with Khoros Communities

Increasing community engagement is a critical but complex challenge. However, with the right community management software, you can streamline and accelerate the process. [Khoros Communities](https://khoros.com/platform/communities) offers advanced features designed to drive deeper engagement — from customizable community spaces where your customers can connect and collaborate, to robust automation tools that keep members engaged and reduce manual work for your team.

With real-time analytics, you’ll gain valuable insights into your community’s activity, helping you identify trends, measure performance, and optimize engagement strategies. Khoros also empowers you to monitor conversations, moderate content effectively, and communicate with your members at scale, creating a more positive and interactive environment.

Ready to unlock the full potential of your online community? [**Request a demo today**](https://khoros.com/demo) and see firsthand how Khoros Communities can help you turn your online space into a thriving, engaged hub.

## Online community engagement FAQs

[#### How to build an engaged online community from the start?](javascript:void(0))

Building an engaged community starts with understanding your audience, setting clear goals, and creating valuable content that sparks conversation. It’s essential to foster a welcoming environment where members feel heard and valued from the very beginning. To learn more about how to build a successful community, check out our blog on [building a community.](https://khoros.com/blog/how-to-build-an-online-community)

[#### How to engage an online community?](javascript:void(0))

Engaging an online community involves creating meaningful interactions, asking thought-provoking questions, offering helpful resources, and acknowledging member contributions. Regularly participating in conversations and using gamification tactics can also help increase engagement.

[#### How can community engagement be improved?](javascript:void(0))

Community engagement can be improved by consistently interacting with members, gathering feedback, personalizing content, and hosting interactive events. It’s important to recognize and reward your most active members to keep the momentum going.

[#### What are examples of good community engagement?](javascript:void(0))

Good community engagement includes hosting live Q&A sessions, sharing user-generated content, responding to feedback, and providing opportunities for members to contribute through polls, challenges, and discussions. The key is making members feel connected and valued.

[#### How can I measure community engagement?](javascript:void(0))

Measuring community engagement can be done through metrics like active participation rates, post interactions (likes, shares, comments), feedback responses, and the frequency of returning users. Analyzing these data points helps you assess the health of your community and optimize your engagement strategies.

--- SOURCE 4: 10 top tips for effective online community management ---
URL: https://www.communityledgrowth.com/top-tips-for-effective-online-community-management/

SUMMARY:
1. Understanding your audience · 2. Setting clear guidelines and expectations · 3. Encouraging engagement and participation · 4. Providing value to

FULL CONTENT:
![](https://pma-assets-external.ams3.cdn.digitaloceanspaces.com/TheAlliance_Assets/alliance-logo-sm.svg)
![Community-Led Alliance](/content/images/size/w600/2024/07/CLA-FULL-LOGO-SECONDARY--ON-WHITE-.png)

# 10 top tips for effective online community management

![Hannah Wesson](https://www.gravatar.com/avatar/1f8da91a8a1f9cb2e465d9dc6c8e3339?s=250&r=x&d=mp)

#### Hannah Wesson

Online communities are vital gathering places where people with shared interests, challenges, and passions come together. Effective online community management is essential, as it ensures that these platforms are hubs of meaningful interaction, growth, and support.

Given virtually every brand and organization has a digital presence of some kind, the ability to sustain an engaged community can provide a competitive edge. It’s not just about monitoring conversations or posting updates; it’s about providing an environment where members feel valued, heard, and connected.

In this article, we’ll delve into tips for community management, and explore the practical steps that can help any community manager excel.

## 1. Understanding your audience

At the heart of any thriving online community lies a deep understanding of its members. Knowing who they are, what they care about, and how they behave within your digital space is paramount.

Demographics such as industry, location, and job title can shape the tone and content of your community interactions, while interests and behaviors provide a roadmap for engagement strategies.

These insights allow you to tailor conversations, content, and community activities to align with members' expectations and preferences, fostering a more personalized and relevant experience for them.

For an even deeper understanding, community managers can leverage analytics tools. They don’t just count the likes and follows; they unravel the complex tapestry of member interaction. Through analytics, community managers can track engagement patterns, identify the most influential voices within the community, and figure out which topics generate the most buzz.

Additionally, insights from analytics tools can help in pinpointing the optimal times for posting content, the types of media that perform best, and the tone that strikes a chord with your audience.

## 2. Setting clear guidelines and expectations

A successful community hinges on having clear guidelines and expectations. These are the rules of engagement that maintain order and foster a positive, constructive environment where everyone can thrive. Clear guidelines serve as a reference point for acceptable behavior, helping to preemptively curb conflict and misunderstandings by setting boundaries.

To craft effective guidelines, start with the core values of your community. What are the non-negotiables that embody the ethos of your platform? From respect and inclusivity to integrity and collaboration, these values should be the foundation of your guidelines. Ensure they are specific, covering key areas such as posting etiquette, privacy expectations, and the process for handling conflict.

Transparency is crucial when communicating these guidelines. They should be easily accessible, perhaps pinned at the top of your community page or included in the welcome message for new members. Regular reminders of these guidelines can also help reinforce their importance.

Enforcing these guidelines is as important as setting them. Develop a clear process for handling violations, which may include warnings, temporary suspensions, or even permanent bans for severe infractions. The enforcement process should be consistent and fair, with community managers and moderators trained to handle situations impartially.

Remember to periodically review and update your community guidelines. As your community evolves, so too may the guidelines need to adapt to new challenges and trends.

## 3. Encouraging engagement and participation

Active participation is the lifeblood of any online community, and fostering this engagement is quite an undertaking. To encourage member involvement, community managers must create opportunities for interaction that are both compelling and rewarding.

### Creating interactive content

Interactive content is key to sparking conversation and participation. This could be in the form of polls, quizzes, or Q&A sessions. By directly involving members in content creation, you give them a stake in the community. Collaborative content series, where members contribute advice or stories, can also be highly engaging.

### Hosting events

Events, whether online webinars, live Q&A sessions, or real-world meetups, can galvanize a community. They offer a platform for members to learn, network, and bond over shared experiences.

### Encouraging user-generated content

User-generated content (UGC), like testimonials, reviews, or community challenges, not only engages members but also creates valuable content for the community. Prompting members to share their experiences or participate in challenges can lead to a richer community experience.

## 4. Providing value to members

To cultivate a loyal and active online community, it's essential to ensure that members perceive real value in their participation. This value can take many forms, from exclusive content and resources to unique opportunities that they wouldn't find elsewhere.

When members feel that being part of the community enriches their lives or careers, their engagement and commitment to the community naturally deepen.

Offering content that is exclusive to community members can be a strong incentive for joining and staying active within the community. This could be in-depth guides, expert interviews, or early access to new products or features.

Providing tools or resources that can help members achieve their goals can add significant value. For example, if you're managing a community of professionals, sharing industry reports, templates, or career development tools could be immensely beneficial.

Creating opportunities for members to learn and grow is another way to provide value. This might include hosting workshops, courses, or mentorship pairings. Encouraging members to share their skills with others not only helps individuals grow but also strengthens the community as a whole.

Offering a platform for networking and collaboration can also be a valuable asset. Members can connect with like-minded individuals for collaboration on projects, sharing of best practices, or simply to form professional connections and friendships.

## 5. Building relationships and trust

The heart of community management isn't found in the content or the events - it's in the personal connections and trust that’s built over time. This human element of trust between members and community managers transforms a mere digital space into a thriving community.

Building personal connections with community members can turn passive users into active participants. When members feel personally invested, they are more likely to contribute, share their experiences, and support each other. This connection is achieved through consistent, genuine interactions that go beyond administrative posts and into the realm of personal engagement.

Building trust requires transparency and authenticity from community managers. Here are some strategies to foster this environment:

## 6. Managing conflicts and moderation

Conflict is an inevitable aspect of any community, and how it’s handled can make or break the trust within the group. Proactive and fair conflict management, supported by a trained moderation team, is essential to maintaining a healthy online environment.

Proactive management involves taking steps to prevent conflicts before they escalate:

When conflicts do arise, they should be addressed swiftly and impartially. Give all parties involved a chance to share their perspective on the situation. Approach the conflict without bias, even if you have a personal opinion, it’s the community manager’s job to be objective.

Apply the community rules uniformly to all members to ensure fairness, and when a resolution is reached, explain the reasoning behind it to the community to maintain transparency.

Every community needs a skilled moderation team to manage conflicts effectively. Choose people who are not only familiar with the community’s subject matter, but that have strong interpersonal skills and good judgment. Ensure they’re trained in conflict resolution, effective communication, and your specific community guidelines.

## 7. Leveraging community feedback

Community feedback is a goldmine of insights that can guide how your community evolves and grows. Actively soliciting, listening to, and implementing feedback demonstrates to members that their opinions are valued and that they have a stake in the community's direction.

### Gathering feedback

There are several effective methods for gathering feedback from community members:

Use these tools to ask specific questions about the community’s features, content, and overall direction. They can be both broad and targeted, depending on the information you're seeking.

Create dedicated spaces or threads where members can leave suggestions and comments about the community.

Some members may prefer to provide feedback privately. Make sure they have a way to send their thoughts directly to you, and ensure you foster an environment where they feel they’re able to reach out directly.

Encourage more members to provide feedback by offering incentives such as badges, recognition, or even tangible rewards.

### Implementing feedback

Merely collecting feedback isn't enough. It must be analyzed and, where appropriate, acted upon.

Let members know that their feedback has been received and is being considered. Review feedback in team meetings to evaluate its feasibility and potential impact on the community. When feedback leads to a change, implement it decisively and communicate the changes back to the community, crediting the feedback where possible. After changes are made, follow up with the community to assess if the feedback has been addressed effectively.

Remember that feedback is not a one-off process, but a continual conversation and an on-going practice.

## 8. Keeping the community evolving

An online community needs to grow and adapt over time to stay fresh and engaging. Stagnation can lead to disengagement, while dynamic evolution can foster renewed interest and commitment from members.

To keep the community alive, introduce new features and activities that match the evolving interests and needs of your members. This could mean rolling out new forum software that allows for better interaction, or creating new content series that tap into current trends within the community's niche.

Regularly scheduled events can also infuse the community with energy. Whether these are themed discussion weeks, guest speaker sessions, Q&As, or collaborative projects, events can give members something to look forward to and participate in.

The evolution of your community should also reflect broader changes in the digital landscape. As new social media platforms emerge and new forms of communication become popular, integrating these into your community will keep it current and accessible.

## 9. Utilizing the right tools and platforms

The digital toolbox available to community managers is vast, with each tool designed to streamline, enhance, or measure community activities. From automation and scheduling to analytics and engagement, these tools can dramatically increase the efficiency and effectiveness of community management.

For automation and scheduling, there are tools like Buffer or Hootsuite. They allow managers to plan content calendars in advance, schedule posts across different time zones, and ensure a consistent stream of communication. Automation can also extend to welcoming new members or moderating content based on set keywords or phrases.

Analytics tools such as Google Analytics offer insights into member behavior and engagement within the community. These tools can track everything from the most visited pages to the paths users take through the site, helping managers understand what content works and what doesn’t.

When choosing the right platform or tool, community managers must consider the specific needs of their community. For instance, a community with members spread across the globe may benefit from sophisticated scheduling tools that ensure content is posted at optimal times for different regions. A smaller, more discussion-focused community might prioritize a robust forum platform with excellent moderation features.

## 10. Measuring success and KPIs

Evaluating the health and growth of an online community requires a clear set of key performance indicators (KPIs). These metrics provide community managers with quantifiable data that reflects the community’s engagement, satisfaction, and expansion.

KPIs vary based on the community's goals, but typical indicators include:

Using these KPIs, community managers can make informed decisions about strategies and initiatives. For instance, if engagement rates are low, it might be necessary to revamp the content strategy or create more opportunities for interaction. If member growth is stagnant, marketing efforts may need to be increased or the onboarding experience improved.

To effectively use data, managers should:

## Final thoughts

Effective online community management is an art that combines a deep understanding of your audience, clear communication of guidelines, proactive engagement, and the creation of meaningful value.

Building authentic relationships, adeptly managing conflicts, and utilizing member feedback are crucial to fostering a thriving community. By following these tips for online community management, community managers can streamline processes, track the right KPIs, and measure the success of their strategies and the health of their community.

# 10 Tactics to Increase Online Community Engagement

Andrea Amorosi

With engagement. In-person and online community engagement are cornerstones of your success as an organization. The latter especially is critical in today’s digital age, but unfortunately, it is too often done wrong. From disorganized messaging to confusing landing pages to ineffective donation strategies, community engagement is a hot mess at many companies, corporations and nonprofits alike. It’s time to do better.

In today’s post, we’re going to take a look at how to do online community engagement right. We’ll take a look at what it is, why it benefits you, and the most effective tactics out there. Ready to see more interaction, greater profits, a happier workforce, and an ever-growing mission? Read on.

## What is online community engagement?

A simple definition of online community engagement is the amount of interaction between your organization and its members in the digital space. Anything that happens between you and them, mediated by a web page or an app, can count.

A more complex definition of online community engagement is an active process in which your organization seeks to deepen relationships between the mission and the people who help serve it – whether that’s through donations, volunteering, purchases of branded gear, promoting your organization on social media or in their own digital spaces, or simply responding to your communications.

As you can see, online community engagement is like an onion. The skin is hardly representative of the whole. Yet many organizations unthinkingly disregard the rich potential of digging deeper into their community engagement, either because they don’t realize they’re doing it or because they don’t know how to do better.

Unfortunately, superficial relationships with your members often result in superficial results for your organization. Understanding the true benefits of a rockin’ engagement strategy can help fix that.

## Benefits of online community engagement

Online community engagement brings with it several important perks, including:

### Build better relationships

When members of your community feel as though they have a voice, they are automatically more invested. They care what *you* have to say because they know you care what *they* have to say.

This also increases their trust in you and their willingness to spread your good name among their own communities, from work to family to hobby groups and beyond. They may even help you forge important connections with other nonprofits or associations that can result in valuable, lifelong partnerships.

### Get more volunteers

One of the best things about an engaged community is your ability to draw in volunteers. Since many groups are run on volunteer work, you can’t afford to ignore this potent source of organizational empowerment. Yet when volunteers feel as though there’s nowhere to go to engage, they may leave for organizations that do give them a platform for sharing their voice, views, and ideas. Don’t let that happen.

### Re-engage disinterested members

The sorry truth is, most members don’t stick around forever. Although you’ll certainly get lifers – people who want to stick with your organization over the long haul – this isn’t the norm. You must accept a certain amount of [churn](https://memberclicks.com/blog/membership-churn/), but that doesn’t mean you have to be fatalistic.

An online community space can help reel in at least a portion of your disengaged membership, helping them reconnect with why they were drawn to your mission in the first place. Make no mistake, these members aren’t the same as new members who have to be wooed, because they’re already used to giving their time, money, and other resources. By virtue of already having been onboarded, they will use up less of *your* resources in return.

### Retain interested members

By the same token, a community space will help you [keep hold of those members who aren’t yet disengaged](https://memberclicks.com/blog/member-retention/), but might become so without easy access to the mission and the message.

### Counter misinformation

The online sphere is famous for promulgating false facts and misinformation (or even disinformation, intentionally seeded to serve a purpose counter to your own). It’s important, especially for any organization that has a cause, to keep on the lookout for misinformation and correct it wherever possible. Your mission relies on it.

Happily, a community engagement platform lets you do just that, giving you a platform on which to speak whenever you want.

### Generate leads

An existing online community engagement platform makes it easy for you to find and generate new leads. Because you already have a place to send interested parties, your funnel is set up for success.

### Seek input

Your members have a lot of valuable input to share, if only you’ll let them. An engagement space lets you seek those opinions through questions, interviews, surveys, polls, and more. While it might feel difficult to adjust the trajectory of your organization based on the thoughts and feelings of others, this is the only way you can know it will survive over the long haul.

Want to nab these benefits at your own organization? Let’s take a look at the following [community member engagement strategy ideas](https://memberclicks.com/blog/member-engagement-strategies-ideas/) now.

## 10 community engagement tactics

Benefits are all well and good, but eventually the rubber has to meet the road. Here are 10 specific, effective, and tested strategies to help you build engagement among your community members today.

### 1. Create a new member experience

One of the best ways to increase online community engagement is to create a new member experience. Chances are your organization already has a community in place, whether it’s small and growing or already thriving.

However, nonprofits and associations can quickly get stagnant. Perhaps they over-rely on strategies that have worked in the past. Maybe there’s nobody on deck who has a marketing background of any kind. Or maybe – and this is especially true for older organizations that were historically successful with analog approaches – they simply haven’t transitioned to the online sphere well.

An online community gathering space can help solve all of these issues. It can bring people together, energize them, and help them solve problems with which they’ve been struggling, such as:

* Confusing payment systems
* Inefficient HR and volunteer coordination strategies
* Disconnect between leadership and community members
* An inability to communicate

### 2. Ask great questions

One of the best ways to increase communication potential is simply to start asking questions, and one of the easiest ways to do that is by using an online platform. As with all other crowdsourcing strategies, an online gathering place helps you solicit public opinion without having to reach out to each member individually. Now you can speak to the masses and give them a voice, but you don’t have to spend the time and manpower to do it.

How can you start asking questions if you’ve never done it before? Simply invite conversation. You can do that in forums, via email links to online feedback forms, or by making yourself readily available on social media.

### 3. Give VIP access

Who doesn’t want to feel a cut above? It’s important to give your members a way to enter the elite circle if they want to, because that will only draw them closer to your organization and mission. This is especially true for people who have put a ton of their own resources (time, money, energy, in-kind donations, networking, hosting) into your cause.

Again, this is a place where online community engagement can help you shine. You can offer VIP access in many ways, including:

* Gated access to a newsletter containing early bird deals or leadership opportunities
* Different subscription or support tiers that come with different gifts
* Entrance into special events, meetings, etc.

### 4. Highlight member successes

Member successes are absolute marketing gold. Not only do they prove your organization works, but they also contain massive media potential.

Member successes can come in many forms. For instance, a member of your writer’s association may land a book deal, in which case you might honor them with a speaking gig at your next event or with an interview on the main page of your website. Alternately, your member success story might come from the community, say from a young single father who was able to send his childhood to a good preschool because of your help. Any story that tugs at the heartstrings or impresses the rest of the community is worth highlighting.

You can then make the most of this by creating different types of content, from [video marketing pieces](https://memberclicks.com/blog/video-marketing-for-associations/) to podcast interviews to written profiles with nice images.

### 5. Spotlight members

Closely related to the above, you’ll want to put a plan in place to spotlight members on your home page. This doesn’t have to comprise only success stories in order to constitute solid online community engagement. Anyone who works hard deserves recognition, even if they didn’t hit the million-dollar mark in a fundraising campaign, get invited to the White House, or cure cancer.

To spotlight members effectively, build a reliable routine. For instance, you might:

1. Decide where on your online member hub the spotlight will go (main page, dedicated page?)
2. Select time periods in which you’ll spotlight people (weekly, monthly, quarterly?)
3. Choose the criteria for picking someone (number of hours worked, different milestones in the community, positive feedback from other members?)
4. Task a member of your staff or volunteer force with conducting interviews and creating media
5. Post regularly on the timeline to which you’ve committed

Doing this will excite people about the potential of recognition, and thus will encourage them to work harder, promote your organization, and stick around for the long haul.

### 6. Moderate your community

It’s so critical to moderate your community once you create it. In order for it to thrive, it must be a positive and loving place for people to interact. Yes, you need meaningful exchanges of ideas, especially important in mission-based work where you are addressing age-old problems that you can’t fix, but can only improve bit by bit.

However, that exchange of ideas has to be respectful. Who is responsible for ensuring it is? You, and only you. You cannot rely on your volunteers, donors, members, or ideologues to do it for themselves. Rather, you must maintain an obvious and caring presence and do it for them. That way, everyone – even the smallest voice – will feel safe participating.

### 7. Diversify your content

Blogs are great, but they’re not everything. True online community engagement means giving *everyone* a way to engage, not just the bookworms of the world.

Remember, not everybody in your community is a strong reader. Indeed, often the community members being served didn’t have the access to communication that we might have hoped for them in childhood. As such, it’s critical you engage with them on wavelengths that resonate. Ideas for moving beyond blogging include:

* Live streaming content from events or simply from HQ
* Videos and podcasts
* Q&A sessions with important people at your organization and in the community
* Interact content
* Gamification

… and more. Take your cue from what people in your community already enjoy and are doing with their time. The more closely you can tailor the digital experience you offer to the ones they seek out as entertainment and/or infotainment, the better you will do.

### 8. Host an amazing event

Now, how to transition your member engagement from the online space to real, in-person events? That’s a quandary for many organizations, who find it challenging to transition from one to the other, with the inevitable result of disjointed communication and often low attendance at live events.

Don’t worry, though: there’s an answer. To increase your community engagement, you need to create a virtuous cycle between both online and physical spaces. At your events, praise and promote your online space and highlight member accomplishments within it. In the online space, talk up your events and offer clear links to signing up, volunteering at, or donating to them.

Stuck for how to put an event together? Here’s a raft of [amazing event engagement ideas](https://memberclicks.com/blog/event-engagement-ideas/) to get you started.

### 9. Community leadership programs

One of the best ways to drive online community engagement is to encourage leadership among your members and volunteers. People want to be leaders for all kinds of reasons, including but not limited to:

* Making a difference
* Improving their resume
* Making amends

No matter why someone wants to give back, leadership is a great way to do that. Offer opportunities for all kinds of sign up methods, for your members to receive the training they’ll need to lead, and routes to get recognition for being a great leader. Best of all, this removes some of the responsibility for delegation from the shoulders of staff.

### 10. Ask your community for feedback

Your organization needs feedback in two ways. First, you need to improve the organization as a whole, which means gathering feedback on such aspects of it as:

* Your mission and whether people find it compelling
* The way you do things, and whether folks think you could do it better
* The effectiveness of your message, your values, your community outreach, and your programs themselves

If you’re sure those are meeting the mark, then it’s time to determine whether your online platform is cutting the mustard. Again, feedback is critical here. While criticism is never fun, input can make your platform a better place to be and engage. This is true for the donors, volunteers, and/or members who make your organization tick.

You can ask for feedback in many ways, such as putting out a poll or survey, conducting interviews with individuals who are willing, and monitoring what happens in online spaces such as forums. You should also ask for feedback consistently over email.

## Questions to help you choose the best online community platform

Each of the above strategies has its own merits, which are powerful indeed. However, you may not get far with any of them if you don’t take the time to choose your online platform wisely. Remember, whether you’re a nonprofit, association, or chamber, your members will be spending a lot of time on your platform. You want it to be a good experience, not a confusing one.

Yes, that means using smart software that reflects your standing as a modern company. But it also means choosing a platform with the capabilities you need to keep members engaged. As such, choosing a community platform is a big decision, and you shouldn’t make it without thoroughly considering its implications for online community engagement. It can help to ask (and answer) a few questions first.

Among the most important of these are:

* What do we need more of: members, content, revenue, or all of the above?
* Where are our current sticking points?
* What holes do we currently have in our online systems?
* Where is member data going to waste?
* How can we put it to better use?
* Which admin tasks go unfulfilled, are confusing, get done poorly or not at all, and require more manpower than they’re worth?
* What online strategies have worked to engage our community members in the past? (E.g. giving campaigns, volunteering signups, surveys, online training, gated content, etc.)
* How have our donors responded differently from our volunteers? Which types of strategy work well for each subset of the community?
* How can we bucket community members effectively so they feel like we’re talking right to them with our strategies?
* What types of experiences do our members prioritize, and how can offering online or in-person experiences get them to engage more readily online?
* What kinds of questions get the most responses from community members?
* What capabilities will an online community need?
* How will answering these questions help us build better connections in the community?

These questions are all important, and sitting down to wrangle some answers with your team will provide more benefits than perhaps you can imagine.  By determining what matters to your members … and what doesn’t … you can help to serve them, which makes them in turn better at serving you and your organization.

Of course, online community engagement won’t go far without the online community engagement solution we discussed above.
